SIMULATION NOTICE: This email is a phishing-awareness training exercise. Do not follow payment or credential requests. (See full notice below.)

Dear Katarina,

Congratulations! You have been selected in the Global Prize Draw and won a cash reward. Our records indicate your registration details (Residence: Munich). This notification is regarding the prize you were selected to receive following the draw on 03 March 2087.

To claim your reward, please complete the verification step and pay a small one-time processing fee so we can initiate the transfer of your prize funds. This is required to confirm identity and expedite transfer procedures.

Claim Your Prize Now

Important (Training): This message is part of an anti-phishing training exercise. DO NOT provide payment or personal credentials. The link above points to a training server (security-training.example.org) and is safe for training observers to inspect without entering personal data.

Reference: TR-2087-4591 • Draw date: 03/03/2087 • Recipient: Katarina (Munich)

If you attempt to follow the original-sounding instructions in a real phishing message, attackers often demand a small fee to 'release' funds or ask for banking details to 'complete the transfer'. This training simulation intentionally mimics those tactics so you can learn to spot them.

You were contacted because an entry associated with your email address was randomly selected. No legitimate prize organization will ever ask you to send money to receive a reward. If this were a real notification and you had concerns, contact your official local authorities or the issuing organization directly using verified channels.

From: "Vanessa Mercer" To: "Terry Williams" Subject: Single & Lonely? Recognize Love Scam Attempts — Training Notice Date: Tue, 09 Sep 2087 21:27:36 +0200 MIME-Version: 1.0 Content-Type: text/html; charset="utf-8" Content-Transfer-Encoding: 7bit Phishing Simulation — Romance Scam Awareness

Cybersecurity Awareness — Simulated Romance Scam

Hi Terry,

This message is a simulated phishing exercise sent by the University Cybersecurity Awareness Team to help students in Hereford spot and avoid romance / "pig butchering" scams. This is not a real attempt to defraud you. The purpose is training only.

Why you received this

Many romance scams try to exploit feelings of being lonely or searching for a connection. Scammers craft messages to tug at the heart and make someone feel special so they can request money or sensitive details. Below is an example of the kind of message these criminals use (simulated).

"Hi Terry — I've felt so lonely lately and my heart reached out when I saw your profile. I feel a special connection to you. Can we meet soon? Please confirm here: http://lovelymeetings-amaz0n-verify.invalid/confirm-2087"

Red flags in the example above:

  • Unexpected declarations of intense emotion (heart, special connection) from a stranger.
  • Pressure to act quickly or to "confirm" via an unfamiliar link.
  • Links to odd or misspelled domains (notice the -amaz0n-verify style in the URL).

If you ever receive a message like the simulated example, do not click links or provide money or personal details. Instead:

  1. Pause — don't reply immediately.
  2. Verify the sender independently (search for official contacts, contact the service directly at a known domain).
  3. Report the message to the University IT Security team.

For step-by-step guidance and a short interactive module (5–7 minutes) on how to spot romance scams, visit our training page:

Start the Romance Scam Module — 2087

Immediate actions

If you believe you have responded to a real scam contact us immediately and do not send money or share bank details. Report suspicious messages to: phish-report@security.example.com

Note: Links in this training email either point to our official training site or to clearly marked example domains (such as .invalid) used for simulation. Never enter credentials on a site you reached from an unexpected link.

Stay safe,

Vanessa Mercer
Cybersecurity Awareness Team — University IT

University IT • Hereford Campus • security.example.com
